records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 390 
3.1 The 1326 CE Institutionalization 
The text documents that prior to the 14th century, individuals maintained 
their own genealogical records (samūha-lekhya). By the 13th century, this 
practice had declined to the point where Paṇḍita Harinatha Upādhyāya, the 
great Dharmaśāstra scholar, unknowingly contracted a prohibited marriage 
with his own paternal cousin's granddaughter. This failure of private 
genealogical memory precipitated a structural crisis. 
In 1326 CE (Śāka 1248), under the impetus of Maharaja Harasiṃhadeva of 
the Karṇāṭa dynasty, the Maithil pandits resolved to transfer genealogical 
record-keeping from private households to a state-sanctioned corps of 
specialists — the Pañjīkāras. This institutionalization corresponds closely to 
what anthropologist Jack Goody theorizes as the transition from 'oral 
genealogy' to 'written genealogy' — a shift that fundamentally alters the 
nature and authority of genealogical claims. 
3.2 Harasiṃhadeva's Scientific Framework 
Harasiṃhadeva's system was remarkable for its egalitarian design: all 
Brahmin, Kāyastha, and Kṣatriya families were recorded identically. The text 
states explicitly: 'Harasiṃhadeva's Pañjī was scientifically grounded 
(vaijñānika ādhāra) and constituted a pure genealogy (śuddha vaṃśāvalī 
paricoaya). All Brahmins, Kāyasthas, and Kṣatriyas were equal in their 
respective Pañjīs.' It was only under Maharaja Mādhava Siṃha (1760 CE) 
that hierarchical ranking (Śrotriya, Yogya, Pañjībaddha, Jayavāra) was 
introduced — a social corruption of a scientific record. 
3.3 The Pañjīkāra as Genealogical Professional 
The text devotes substantial attention to the Pañjīkāra as a professional 
genealogist. 185 Pañjīkāras are documented: 32 serving the Śrotriya stratum 
and 153 serving Yogya and Vaṃśaja strata. The text decries the 
commercialization of the profession and calls for its ethical reform: 'The 
Pañjīkāra is a guardian of culture, not a petty trader.' This resonates with 
contemporary debates in professional genealogical ethics about the 
responsibilities of record-keepers.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 391 
IV. Kinship Theory and the Sapiṇḍa Algorithm 
The Pañjī system's marriage-eligibility algorithm — the Uteḍha with its 16 
Naṭṭha (ancestral positions) — constitutes one of the most mathematically 
precise kinship prohibition systems in pre-modern social organization 
anywhere in the world. 
4.1 The 32-Ancestor Matrix 
The Uteḍha requires mapping 32 ancestors for both bride and groom: 16 
patrilineal and 16 matrilineal for each party. The candidate must be in the 
'sixth position' (ṣaṭha sthāna) or more removed from all 16 of the Naṭṭha on 
the paternal side; matrilineal Naṭṭha are calculated separately. This 
corresponds mathematically to a prohibition covering a 5-generation 
matrilineal and 7-generation (later 6-generation) patrilineal exclusion zone 
— a formula enshrined in Manu and Yājñavalkya and refined by Maheśa 
Ṭhākura. 
4.2 Comparison with Alliance Theory 
Lévi-Strauss's alliance theory posited that marriage prohibition systems are 
fundamentally about the exchange of women between groups to create social 
solidarity. The Pañjī system operates differently: its prohibitions are framed 
in terms of sapiṇḍa (shared ritual body) rather than alliance, and are bilateral 
(covering both maternal and paternal lines), making it closer to what Goody 
calls 'diverging devolution' systems than to the classic prescriptive alliance 
systems of Lévi-Strauss. 
Yet the Pañjī's social function does produce alliance: the 16 Naṭṭha of each 
family become, in effect, a web of known relationships, and the Pañjīkāra's 
Siddhānta-patra certifies the absence of sapiṇḍa as a precondition for forming 
new alliances. The system thus governs alliance through the management of 
descent records — an elegant inversion of Lévi-Strauss's model. 
4.3 The Chromosomal Analogy 
The text invokes a striking analogy, comparing the Uteḍha's 32-ancestor 
matrix to chromosomal mapping: 'In the current age of science, the 
determination of chromosomes (guṇasūtra) is of this type — our forebears 
(Ṛṣis) placed this in a systematic manner long before.' This is not naive 
scientism. The comparison is apt: the sapiṇḍa prohibition is, at its biological

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 392 
base, a mechanism for reducing inbreeding coefficients. The 5th/7th 
generation prohibition corresponds roughly to a kinship coefficient below 
1/128, approaching the threshold at which most genetic concerns about 
consanguinity become negligible. 
This anticipation of population-genetic logic — articulated through 
Dharmaśāstra centuries before modern genetics — is among the most 
theoretically significant aspects of the Pañjī system. 
4.4 The Four Prohibition Rules 
The Pañjī system encodes four explicit marriage prohibition rules, grounded 
in Manusmṛti and Yājñavalkya: 
• 
Rule 1 (Sapiṇḍa): The bride and groom must not share any of the 16 
Naṭṭha in the groom's patrilineal descent. 
• 
Rule 2 (Gotra): They must not belong to the same gotra. 
• 
Rule 3 (Pravara): They must not share the same pravara (ancestral Ṛṣi). 
• 
Rule 4 (Vimatā): The bride must not be the daughter of the groom's 
stepmother's brother. 
These rules form a multi-layered prohibition system covering genetic, ritual, 
and social dimensions simultaneously — a sophistication unmatched in 
comparative systems. 
V. Documentary vs. Oral Genealogy: The Pañjī's Textual 
Architecture 
Genealogical theory distinguishes between oral genealogies (transmitted 
through memory, recitation, and performance) and documentary genealogies 
(preserved in written archives). The Pañjī system is unusual in being both 
simultaneously. 
5.1 The Transition from Samūha-Lekhya to Institutional Archive 
Kumārila Bhaṭṭa's 7th-century Tantravārttika records the existence of 
samūha-lekhya — collective written genealogical records maintained by elite 
families. The 1326 CE institutionalization transformed this private practice 
into a public archive. The text's account of this transition — from individual 
family records to the Pañjīkāra-managed corpus — tracks precisely what 
Clanchy (in 'From Memory to Written Record') identifies as the key 
medieval transition in record culture.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 393 
5.2 The Tirhuta Script as Genealogical Medium 
All Pañjī manuscripts are written in Tirhutā (Maithilī) script — a fact of 
immense significance for understanding the relationship between script, 
community, and genealogical knowledge. The text argues that the Pañjī is 
effectively the lifeblood of Tirhutā script: 'The Pañjī is the prana (vital 
breath) of Tirhutā script.' The inaccessibility of the script to outsiders has 
simultaneously preserved the Pañjī and limited its scholarly reception — a 
classic paradox of archival exclusivity. 
5.3 The Palm-Leaf Corpus 
The physical archive consists of palm-leaf manuscripts (tālapatra), each book 
comprising 500-600 leaves. The text describes in precise detail the 
preparation, preservation, and organization of these manuscripts — including 
the annual sun-drying ritual (when the sun is in Siṃha Rāśi, approximately 
August 16 - September 15) and the injunction to protect manuscripts from 
water, oil, and physical binding. The oldest surviving manuscripts referenced 
include Pā. Raghudev Jhā's Māṇḍar mūla book (1690-1710 CE) and Pā. 
Devānanda Jhā's 'Nhoṭī' branch book (c. 1770-1780 CE). 
The work begun in 2000 CE (Chaitra month) to digitize and transliterate this 
corpus — converting Tirhutā script into Devanagari — represents a critical 
archival intervention: the first systematic transfer of the Pañjī's genealogical 
data from a restricted manuscript tradition into a publicly accessible digital 
medium. 
5.4 The Śākhā Pañjī as Genealogical Literature 
The text claims that the Śākhā Pañjī is 'an unprecedented book — no such 
book has been found in any country, in any sect, or in any class anywhere in 
the world.' This claim invites comparison with the Welsh bardic genealogies, 
the Irish Book of Leinster, the Polynesian 'aro (genealogical recitations), and 
the Japanese kakochō. While none of these match the Pañjī's scale (1, years 
of continuous documentation across an entire region's population), the 
comparison illuminates the Pañjī's distinctiveness: it is not a dynastic 
genealogy but a total societal genealogy.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 394 
VI. The Genome Mapping Framework: Genealogy as Bio-
Social Science 
The text's most ambitious theoretical move is its framing of the Pañjī as a 
form of 'Genome Mapping' — a term Thakur uses deliberately and with 
precision. 
6.1 Two-Phase Mapping 
The text distinguishes between Genome Mapping (Phase 1, covering the 
three Khaṇḍas of the ancient Pañjī corpus) and 'Genealogical Mapping' 
(Phase 2), which constitutes 'Family History' — the foundation of any 
genomic study. This two-phase structure maps onto contemporary genomic 
methodology: population-level genetic data requires individual family history 
as its baseline. 
6.2 Chronological Calibration via Generational Count 
One of the text's most impressive achievements is its use of the Pañjī's 
generational records to calibrate historical chronology. Using a 40-year 
generation length, the text demonstrates: 
• 
Āryabhaṭṭa (Ādibbhaṭṭa, of Māṇḍar-Kāśyapa gotra) is placed at 
generation 39 from the compilation point, yielding a birth date of 
approximately 476 CE — matching historical consensus. 
• 
Goṇū Jhā (Karmahe Sonkariyām, Vatsa gotra) is placed at generation 24, 
yielding approximately 1050-1150 CE. 
• 
Vidyāpati Ṭhakkura (Viṣaevara, Bisfī-Kāśyapa) is placed at generation 
14 from compilation, yielding approximately 1350-1435 CE — again 
matching historical consensus. 
This use of generational depth to independently date historical figures is a 
sophisticated application of what genealogists call 'computational genealogy' 
— cross-validating documentary and demographic methods. 
6.3 The Pañjī as Population Database 
The text's claim that 'no human genealogy spread over such a vast territory 
has been recorded so comprehensively for more than 1, years, in the way the 
people of Mithilā have done' is substantiated by its statistics: 20 gotras, 167 
mūlas (principal mūlas: 35, organized in three hierarchical tiers), 185 
Pañjīkāras, spanning a territory from Nepal to Bangladesh. By any measure,

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 395 
the Pañjī constitutes one of the largest continuous human genealogical 
databases in world history. 
VII. The Dūṣaṇa Pañjī: A Counter-Archive and Subaltern 
Genealogy 
The titular Dūṣaṇa Pañjī — the Register of Defects — is theoretically the 
most subversive component of the system. Its 97 tabulated entries constitute 
a genealogical counter-archive that documents what the normative system 
sought to suppress. 
7.1 What the Dūṣaṇa Pañjī Records 
The tabulated entries in the Dūṣaṇa Pañjī record marriages across class, 
caste, and community boundaries: 
• 
Marriages into 'unknown' lineages (ajñāta vaṃśa) — families whose 
ancestry could not be verified. 
• 
Marriages into Dalit communities (e.g., Camār, specified directly in 
entries such as 'Valliyāsa Camaru suta Gaṅgādhar kī kanyā meṃ'). 
• 
Marriages with Devadāsī women (explicitly documented in entries 17-21 
— Jānika Devadāsa records). 
• 
Marriages into Muslim families ('Daulatijahāṃ' appearing in entries 11-
12). 
• 
Marriages with women of unknown or inter-caste origin ('aṃtajātīya'). 
• 
Marriages into Koch Rajvanshi and other non-Maithil royal families. 
The text provides specific genealogical tables for 97 such cases, each tracing 
the descent from the 'transgressive' union across multiple generations to show 
the contemporary family members descended from it. 
7.2 The Politics of Disclosure 
The Dūṣaṇa Pañjī's political logic is articulated explicitly: disclosure of these 
unions is postponed until 'kālātīta' — after the events have become 
temporally remote enough that their disclosure will not harm the living 
descendants. As the text states: 'When an event becomes kālātīta, it will have 
no adverse effect on any lineage; rather, it will serve as guidance for what 
decision should be taken in a similar situation.'

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 396 
From the perspective of subaltern genealogical theory, this temporal logic is 
double-edged. On one hand, it protects Dalit and marginalized women and 
their descendants from contemporary stigma. On the other, it defers the full 
disclosure of Brahminic social mixing indefinitely, allowing the normative 
system to maintain the fiction of purity while quietly absorbing transgressive 
unions into its documented history. 
7.3 Thakur's Subaltern Reading 
Thakur foregrounds the Dūṣaṇa Pañjī's evidence of inter-caste mixing as 
proof of what he calls the fundamental liberalism of Maithil society: 'The 
society that is described as Jayavāra has, since ancient times, been more 
liberal and tolerant compared to the other civilizations and cultures of India. 
No form of religious fanaticism has been a permanent feature here.' 
This reading deploys the genealogical archive against Brahminic self-
representation: the same Pañjī system that codified caste purity also 
documented, with ethnographic fidelity, the pervasive reality of caste 
transgression. The Dūṣaṇa Pañjī is thus a deconstructive genealogy — an 
archive that, in recording the exceptions to the rule, undermines the rule's 
claim to absolute authority. 
7.4 Devadāsī Genealogy and Female Erasure 
A significant sub-theme of the Dūṣaṇa Pañjī concerns Devadāsī women and 
their children. Entries 17-21 document the children of Devadāsīs who are 
'ajñāta mānala gelīh' (considered unknown/unnamed) yet whose descendants 
are 'samādṛta' (respected) in contemporary society. This pattern — the 
deliberate erasure of the mother's identity while preserving the patriline's 
record — reflects what Gerda Lerner identifies as the foundational 
mechanism of patriarchal genealogy: the invisibility of women's reproductive 
labor within the official record. 
Critically, the text notes that 'only in the Rasāḍha-Arrariyā Pañjī are women's 
names recorded.' This single exception illuminates the rule: the Pañjī system 
is structured around paternal descent, with women appearing only as 
relational terms (māna, mātrika, dou — daughter-in-law, mother's lineage, 
daughter). The Dūṣaṇa Pañjī's documentation of Devadāsī mothers is thus a 
partial recovery of erased female genealogy — partial because the mothers' 
names are still suppressed.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 397 
VIII. The Gotra-Pravara System: Vedic Genealogy and Its 
Classification 
The Pañjī's classification of Maithil Brahmins through gotra and pravara 
represents a Vedic genealogical taxonomy that predates the Pañjī-Prabandha 
by millennia. 
8.1 The 20 Gotras 
The text enumerates all 20 gotras of Maithil Brahmin society, each named 
after a primordial Vedic sage (Śāṇḍilya, Vatsa, Sāvarṇa, Kāśyapa, Parāśara, 
Bhāradvāja, 
Kātyāyana, 
Garga, 
Kauśika, 
Alāmbukākṣa, 
Kṛṣṇāneya, 
Gautama, 
Maudgalya, 
Vaśiṣṭha, 
Kauṇḍinya, 
Upamanyū, 
Kapila, 
Viṣṇuvṛddhi, Taṇḍi, and Jātūkarṇa). This system represents a genealogical 
taxonomy in which all members of a gotra claim descent from a common 
primordial ancestor — precisely the 'segmentary lineage' structure theorized 
by Evans-Pritchard. 
8.2 Pravara: The Sub-Lineage System 
Pravara further subdivides gotras by identifying the 2-5 Ṛṣi ancestors who 
composed Ṛgvedic hymns. Dvipravara families (those with 2 ancestral Ṛṣis) 
and Pañcapravara (5 ancestral Ṛṣis) families cannot intermarry even if their 
gotras differ — since Vātsa and Sāvarṇa share the same Ṛṣi ancestors (Ova, 
Cyavana, Bhārgava, Jāmadagnya, Āplāvāna), marriages between them are 
prohibited despite their nominal gotra distinction. 
The text provides the complete pravara list for all 20 gotras — a taxonomic 
resource of exceptional genealogical precision. 
8.3 The Pañca Gauḍa Classification 
The macro-genealogy of Brahmin communities across India is organized into 
two groups: Pañca Gauḍa (north of the Vindhyas: Utkal, Kānyakubja, Gauḍa, 
Maithil, Sārasvata) and Pañca Draviḍa (south of the Vindhyas: Kārnāṭa, 
Gujara, Tailaṅga, Draviḍa, Māhārāṣṭrīya). Maithil Brahmins are thus located 
within this macro-taxonomy as one of the five northern sub-groups — a 
classification that reflects the political-geographical organization of 
Brahminic culture in the post-Vedic period.

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 398 
IX. Comparative Genealogical Theory: The Pañjī in Global 
Context 
Situating the Pañjī within the global history of genealogical systems 
illuminates both its uniqueness and its theoretical significance. 
9.1 Comparison with Medieval European Genealogy 
Medieval European genealogical systems (Carolingian royal genealogies, the 
Welsh Triads, Domesday Book entries) served primarily dynastic and 
property functions. The Pañjī differs fundamentally: it is a total population 
genealogy covering all social strata (not just elites), maintained for 
ritual/marriage purposes rather than political legitimation, and organized 
around the principle of sapiṇḍa prohibition rather than inheritance. 
9.2 Comparison with Chinese Genealogy (Zupu) 
Chinese clan genealogies (zupu) are the closest global parallel to the Pañjī in 
terms of scope and continuity. Like the Pañjī, they record multiple 
generations of a clan's patrilineal descent. Unlike the Pañjī, they are 
maintained by the clans themselves rather than by an independent 
professional corps, and they do not function as a real-time marriage 
eligibility certification system. The Pañjī's institutional structure — with 
state-appointed Pañjīkāras issuing legally recognized Siddhānta-patra — has 
no parallel in the Chinese system. 
9.3 Comparison with Polynesian Genealogy 
Polynesian genealogical recitations (like the Hawaiian Kumulipo) share the 
Pañjī's ambition of total genealogical coverage, but are primarily oral and 
cosmogonic — tracing descent from divine origins. The Pañjī is explicitly 
empirical 
and 
terrestrial, 
beginning 
its 
documented 
record 
from 
approximately 450 CE with named, historically verifiable individuals. 
9.4 The Pañjī's Unique Features 
In comparative perspective, the Pañjī system is distinguished by four features 
that have no global parallel in combination: 
• 
Continuous documentation spanning 1,500+ years across an entire 
regional population.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 399 
• 
Independent professional genealogists (Pañjīkāras) functioning as state-
authorized certifiers. 
• 
A mathematically precise kinship coefficient algorithm (the 32-ancestor 
Uteḍha) for marriage eligibility. 
• 
A counter-archive (the Dūṣaṇa Pañjī) that simultaneously documents 
transgression against and within the normative system. 
X. Critical Perspectives: Genealogy, Power, and Reform 
A complete genealogical study of the Pañjī cannot avoid its implication in 
structures of social power. Thakur's text is itself a critical intervention, and 
its critique must be incorporated into the theoretical framework. 
10.1 The Brahminic Capture of a Scientific System 
Thakur's most 
sustained 
critique 
concerns 
the 
transformation 
of 
Harasiṃhadeva's egalitarian genealogical system into a vehicle for 
Brahminic social stratification. The introduction of the Śrotriya-Yogya-
Pañjībaddha-Jayavāra hierarchy by Maharaja Mādhava Siṃha and court 
Brahmins converted the Pañjī from a scientific record into a ranking 
instrument. 'The classification of Śrotriya and others,' the text argues, 'began 
from the era of Maharaja Mādhava Siṃha and the practice of Śākhā Pañjī, 
and the harmful social practices arising from this ranking hierarchy began.' 
10.2 Commercialization and Ethical Decay 
The text denounces the commercialization of the Pañjīkāra's function: 
demanding advance payment before issuing eligibility determinations, 
charging each person separately for a single group task, and prioritizing 
revenue over accuracy. This critique maps onto Bloch's analysis of how 
genealogical specialists across cultures face the tension between their civic 
function (as custodians of public knowledge) and their private interests (as 
holders of a specialized, monetizable skill). 
10.3 The Reform Program 
Thakur proposes a reform of the Pañjī system grounded in its original 
Dharmaśāstra logic. He argues that the Uteḍha's 16 Naṭṭha are equally 
weighted — no Naṭṭha has special priority over another — and that the 
asymmetry between the Śrotriya's full Śākhā recitation and the ordinary

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 400 
person's Naṭṭha-based eligibility determination is a post-facto social 
invention with no Dharmaśāstra basis. The reform program calls for 
returning to the system's original scientific function: genealogical 
certification of marriage eligibility based on sapiṇḍa calculation alone. 
10.4 Digital Archival Theory and the Pañjī's Future 
The text's account of the digitization project begun in 2000 CE raises 
questions central to digital archival theory. The transliteration of Tirhutā 
manuscripts into Devanagari Maithili represents a script-crossing that 
changes the Pañjī's semiotic register: from a specialist's script accessible only 
to trained Pañjīkāras, to a common Devanagari text accessible to any literate 
person. 
This 
democratization 
of 
access 
is 
simultaneously 
a 
decontextualization — separating the genealogical data from the specialized 
knowledge tradition that made it interpretable. 
The text advocates for the digital availability of the Pañjī in 'the script most 
widely used among the majority of people' — recognizing that the system's 
survival in the age of globalization requires its translation into publicly 
accessible formats, even at the cost of some specialist knowledge. 
XI. Navya-Nyāya Epistemology of the Pañjī: Genealogy as 
Inference 
Gajendra Thakur's broader scholarly engagement with Navya-Nyāya 
philosophy — particularly the epistemology of Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya — 
provides a distinctive theoretical lens for reading the Pañjī system. From the 
Navya-Nyāya perspective, genealogical knowledge is a form of anumāna 
(inference) structured by specific rules of evidence and certification. 
11.1 Pratyakṣa and Anumāna in the Pañjī 
The Pañjī system distinguishes between direct testimony (the Pañjīkāra's 
personal knowledge of a lineage), recorded evidence (the written Pañjī texts), 
and inferred eligibility (the Siddhānta-patra issued on the basis of this 
evidence). This structure maps onto Navya-Nyāya's epistemological 
categories: pratyakṣa (direct perception), anumāna (inference), and śabda 
(testimony). The Pañjīkāra's function is precisely anumāna: from the written 
record of 32 ancestors, the eligibility of a marriage is inferred.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 401 
11.2 The Uteḍha as Logical Schema 
The Uteḍha's 16-Naṭṭha calculation can be formalized as a logical schema in 
Navya-Nyāya terms. Each Naṭṭha is a vyāpti (universal relation) of kinship; 
the Siddhānta-patra is the conclusion (sādhya) derived from the absence of 
any such vyāpti in the groom's patrilineal descent. The Pañjīkāra performs 
what Gaṅgeśa would recognize as a pramāṇa — a valid means of knowledge 
— grounded in documented genealogical evidence. 
XII. Conclusion: The Pañjī as a Theoretical Paradigm 
Gajendra Thakur's Dūṣaṇa Pañjī / The Black Book presents the Maithila 
Pañjī system as a complete genealogical paradigm — one that integrates 
biological, 
social, 
institutional, 
documentary, 
and 
epistemological 
dimensions into a single coherent system. From the perspective of 
genealogical theory, it constitutes: 
• 
A kinship system of exceptional mathematical precision, anticipating 
modern genetic understanding of consanguinity. 
• 
An institutional archive of unparalleled continuity and scope, rivaling 
any documentary genealogical system in world history. 
• 
A counter-archive (the Dūṣaṇa Pañjī) that documents the reality of inter-
caste mixing, functioning as a subaltern genealogical record within and 
against the normative system. 
• 
A site of ongoing social critique: Thakur's text uses the Pañjī's own 
evidence to contest the Brahminic hierarchy that has accumulated around 
it. 
• 
A model for digital genealogical archival, as the ongoing transliteration 
project attempts to transfer 1, years of manuscript genealogy into a 
publicly accessible digital format. 
The Pañjī is not a relic. It remains a living institution — issuing marriage 
eligibility certificates to contemporary Maithil families, recording new births 
and marriages, and continuing the unbroken genealogical record that began, 
according to the text's earliest traced entries, with Niniyana Bhaṭṭa 
(approximately 400 CE). Its theoretical significance for comparative 
genealogical studies, kinship anthropology, population genetics, archival 
science, and subaltern history has been systematically undervalued in 
scholarship outside the Maithila tradition.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 402 
Thakur's work is a corrective to that neglect. In framing the Pañjī as 'Genome 
Mapping,' he insists on its place not only in South Asian social history but in 
the global history of human attempts to know, record, and regulate the 
connections between generations.

I. Introduction and Nature of the Source 
The Dooshan Panji — literally the 'Defect Register' or 'Black Book' — is one 
of the most remarkable archival documents in the history of Indian 
genealogical traditions. It constitutes the fifth of seven canonical types of 
Panji 
(genealogical 
register) 
maintained 
among 
Maithil 
Brahmin 
communities of the Mithila region (modern Bihar and the Tarai of Nepal). 
Unlike the primary Mool Panji (root register), the Shakha Panji (branch 
register), the Gotra Panji (lineage register), the Patra Panji (issue register), 
and the Utedh Panji (sapinda-extinction register), the Dooshan Panji served 
the specific function of recording departures from normative endogamy — 
inter-caste unions, sexual irregularities, widow remarriage, marriages with

Gajendra Thakur — 404 
devadasi or artisan-caste women — and tracking the generational 
consequences for lineage status. 
The document made available for this research is the Mithilakshar-to-
Devanagari transliteration undertaken from the year 2000 CE onwards by 
Gajendra Thakur, editor of Videha eJournal. This transliteration drew upon 
palm-leaf and bark manuscripts (tarapatra and basaha patra) maintained by 
professional genealogists (Panjikar families) over approximately 1, years. 
The archive comprises upwards of 11, palm-leaf images (available on DVD 
and digitally at videha.co.in). The Dooshan Panji section relevant here was 
released publicly in 2009, with the Tables (1–97) constituting the primary 
empirical dataset examined in this report. 
The document is simultaneously a genealogical archive, a sociological 
record, a caste-mobility ledger, and — as demonstrated through its central 
case study — a suppressed biographical record of Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya (c. 
13th–14th century CE), the founding philosopher of the Navya-Nyāya school 
of Indian logic. Its significance extends far beyond the regional: it is a 
primary source document for the history of Indian philosophy, caste, and the 
sociology of knowledge. 
II. The Panji System: Structure and Historiography 
II.1 Institutional Origins 
The Panji-Prabandha system was formally institutionalised under King 
Harasimhadeva (born 1294 CE, acceded 1307 CE) of the Karnat dynasty of 
Mithila. 
As 
recorded 
in 
Jyotirishwar 
Thakur's 
Varna-Ratnakara, 
Harasimhadeva appointed Gunakar Jha as the first genealogist (Panjikar) for 
Maithil Brahmins, Shankardatt for Karna Kayasthas, and Vijaydatt for 
Kshatriyas. Following Harasimhadeva's flight to Nepal in 1324–25 CE after 
defeat by Ghiyasuddin Tughlaq, Mithila scholars fixed the commencement of 
the current Panji-Prabandha at 1248 Shaka / 1326 CE. This date is 
corroborated by internal Panji evidence. 
A later and decisive restructuring occurred when Mithilesh Maharaja 
Madhav Singh, around 1760 CE, ordered the compilation of Shakha Pustaks 
(branch books). This event precipitated the emergence of the Shrotriya sub-
caste — a crucial sociological datum: as demonstrated by Gajendra Thakur's 
analysis of authentic Panji files, the Shrotriya sub-caste did not exist in

British India before approximately 1800 CE and does not exist among 
Maithil Brahmins in the Nepal portion of Mithila even today. This 
demolishes retrospective claims of immemorial Shrotriya identity. 
II.2 Typology of Panji Registers 
The seven canonical types illuminate the system's comprehensive scope: 
Mool Panji | Primary genealogical register; uniform in original form across 
all branches 
Shakha Panji | Branch registers for Sochhat, Jog, and Panjibadhha sub-
groups; includes Uptip (Sochhatpura) and Nagram (Pachiwar Par) 
Gotra Panji | Records all gotras (exogamous lineage groups) and their 
ancient Mool (root villages) 
Patra Panji | Records descendants (apatya); in use approximately 500 years; 
introduces Mool-gram reference 
Dooshan Panji | Records genealogical 'defects' (ksharan) — the central 
subject of this report 
Utedh Panji | Records sapinda extinction and known ancestors across 
generations 
Aswajan-ya Patra Panji | Supplementary register for affinal kin; sole register 
(Rasadh-Araria) to record women's names 
The Dooshan Panji's revelatory character lies in its documentation that inter-
caste liaisons, marriages outside varna, and relationships with devadasi, 
artisan, and Dom communities were sufficiently frequent to require a 
dedicated institutional record. The very existence of the register proves that 
Mithila's Brahmin community was neither as endogamous nor as caste-rigid 
as later canonical historiography — and Sahitya Akademi-sanctioned 
scholarship — has claimed. 
II.3 Anshuman Pandey's Independent Corroboration 
The significance and reliability of these records are corroborated by 
independent academic use. Anshuman Pandey (University of Michigan, 
doctoral dissertation 2014: 'Recasting the Brahmin in Medieval Mithila: 
Origins of Caste Identity among the Maithil Brahmins of North Bihar') 
received a complete digitised set of Panji records from Gajendra Thakur in 
2007, noting that Panjikar families — who regard these records as 
intellectual property — are typically reluctant to share them. Pandey's 
dissertation deployed these records to reconstruct the historical formation of

independent scholarly validation of the archive's authenticity and analytical

III. Anthropological Analysis 
III.1 The Panji as a Total Social Institution 
In Marcel Mauss's sense of a 'total social fact' — one that simultaneously 
expresses religious, juridical, moral, economic, aesthetic, and morphological 
dimensions — the Panji system constitutes what may be called a Total Social 
Institution. It is not merely a genealogical register but the constitutive 
framework of social personhood in Mithila. Membership in the Panji is the 
condition of possibility for marriage, religious participation, property 
inheritance, and judicial standing within the community. 
The Dooshan Panji, in particular, functions as what Erving Goffman would 
term a stigma register: a master document that attaches spoiled identity to 
lineages across generations. However — and this is the document's most 
anthropologically surprising feature — it simultaneously provides the 
mechanism for purification: the Tables systematically track the generational 
restoration of ritual status (the concept of Purnatah Shuddha, 'completely 
pure,' achieved by the sixth generation in the primary case studied, namely 
Example 1 in Table 1). 
III.2 Kinship Analysis: Cross-Caste Marriages and Hypogamy 
The 97 tables of the Dooshan Panji constitute a dataset of inter-caste and 
socially transgressive marriages documented over approximately 1, years. 
The following patterns emerge from a systematic reading: 
• 
Hypogamic unions (Brahmin men with lower-caste women) are recorded 
but relatively rare; hypergamic unions (lower-caste men with Brahmin 
women) are also present. 
• 
Marriages with Charmakar (leather-worker / tanner) women: most 
prominently in the case of Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya's mother and, crucially, 
his wife (see Section V). 
• 
Unions with devadasi (temple-dancers): multiple entries record maternal 
lineages connected to devadasi women; Examples 16, 17, 18, 20, 21

document children of devadasi mothers across the Shodarpura, Khowal, 
Tilaya, and Budwal Mool lineages. 
• 
Marriages with Rajak (washerman-caste) women: Tables 54, 55, 64, 65, 
66, 67 document inter-marriages between Brahmin men and Rajak 
women, with their genealogical ramifications tracked through multiple 
generations. 
• 
Marriages with artisan castes (Charmakar/Charmakarin): Tables 49–51 
explicitly name Charmakarin as maternal ancestor, with the lineage of 
Gaṅgeśa directly implicated. 
• 
Unions with 'Antajatiya' persons (those of the lowest birth): Tables 45, 
46, 47 record such unions without stigmatising them as final; the 
documents continue to track the lineages. 
• 
A unique entry (Table 49, Entry 188/2) records a woman identified as 
Charmakarin from Babhaniyam (Bhabanipur) as the wife of a man 
identified with the Chhadan Mool — the lineage entry immediately 
preceding the explicit naming of Gaṅgeśa. 
• 
Unions with Muslim women and widows who remarried: the editorial 
commentary in the document explicitly states this is among the 
categories the Dooshan Panji records. 
From an anthropological standpoint, what is most significant is not the 
transgression itself but the institutional response: the Dooshan Panji neither 
condemns nor excommunicates. It tracks, it records, it projects forward. It is 
an instrument of social memory that maintains knowledge while managing 
the social distance required to prevent permanent ritual pollution. The 
mechanism of generational purification (Shuddhi in six generations) reflects 
a sophisticated, flexible caste logic — far more adaptive than later colonial-
period rigidification of caste identity would suggest. 
III.3 The Question of 'Burning Questions' (Dalilit Karnewali 
Ghatna) 
The Dooshan Panji's editorial apparatus — reflected in Gajendra Thakur's 
framing — explicitly deploys the concept of a 'Burning Question': an event 
so socially disturbing that it agitates society and intellect for a brief period 
before being forgotten (kaalatit bhay vismart hoit rahal). The document 
argues that Mithila's social memory was paradoxically both long (sustained 
by Panji archives for 1, years) and systematically amnesiac (events forgotten 
once the social crisis passed). This is a precise articulation of what Maurice

Halbwachs would call the selective mechanism of collective memory, and 
what Paul Connerton calls 'forgetting as a social performance.' 
The Panji system thus preserves what social forgetting seeks to erase. The 
Dooshan Panji is, in Connerton's terms, an 'inscribed memory' that survives 
the 'incorporated memory' (bodily, social, habitual) of the communities 
whose practices it records. 
IV. Sociological Analysis 
IV.1 Caste as Dynamic Process: Against the Colonial-Static Model 
The dominant model of Indian caste — inherited from colonial ethnography 
(Risley, Blunt, Hutton) and its successors — treats caste as a fundamentally 
static, ascriptive, and hypergamically closed system. The Dooshan Panji 
fundamentally challenges this model. The document reveals caste in Mithila 
as a dynamic, processual, and negotiable social category in which: 
• 
Inter-caste unions were sufficiently common to require dedicated 
institutional documentation across 97 cases. 
• 
The consequences of such unions were tracked not as permanent 
excommunication but as generational trajectories capable of restoration. 
• 
The genealogical record itself served as a form of social insurance, 
protecting the main lineage from 'ill-effects' (dushparinaam) while 
preserving knowledge of the departed branch. 
• 
Knowledge of past 'defects' enabled communities to make informed 
judgements about contemporary social situations — a form of what we 
might call genealogical risk management. 
This dynamic model resonates with Louis Dumont's recognition in Homo 
Hierarchicus (1966) that the opposition of pure and impure is not a fixed 
binary but a relational gradient — what matters is relative position, not 
absolute pollution. The Dooshan Panji institutionalises precisely this 
relational, processual understanding of purity. 
IV.2 Gender and the Register: Women's Bodies as Genealogical 
Sites 
The Dooshan Panji reveals a gendered sociology of genealogical recording. 
The document notes that only the Rasadh-Araria Panji records women's 
names at all — the other registers trace patrilineal descent and identify

records.text:

Gajendra Thakur — 409 
women primarily as maternal roots (matrik) of their sons. This androcentrism 
is structurally significant: the 'defect' in genealogical terms is always defined 
in relation to the male line, yet it is the female body — the mother, wife, or 
daughter — through whom transgression enters and purification must work. 
The repeated formula 'Mata Agyat' (Mother Unknown) — appearing in 
dozens of entries across the 97 tables — is sociologically revealing. It 
signifies not ignorance but deliberate suppression: the identity of mothers 
from lower castes or devadasi backgrounds was systematically withheld from 
the main genealogical record while being preserved in the Dooshan register. 
This is a gendered double inscription: the main register erases; the Dooshan 
register preserves. 
The devadasi entries deserve particular attention. Examples 16–21 in the 
Tables document multiple lineages where the maternal grandmother or great-
grandmother was a devadasi. The document neither pathologises this 
connection nor erases it — it records it with the same precision applied to all 
other genealogical facts. This suggests that the Mithila devadasi institution 
(connected, as elsewhere in India, to temple service) was not regarded as 
placing women entirely outside the sphere of genealogically significant 
social relationships. 
IV.3 Mobility, Migration, and the Sociology of 'Paschhim Desh Se 
Agata' 
Several entries in the Dooshan Panji record individuals described as arriving 
'from the Western direction' (Paschim Desh se Aagat) — a formula 
indicating migration into the Mithila genealogical territory from outside. 
These entries pose a significant sociological question: how were outsiders 
incorporated into the Panji system? The document suggests that 
incorporation required tracing descent (or acknowledging its absence) and 
that certain transgressive unions were precisely with persons of unclear 
lineage — 'unknown caste and community' (Agyat Vansh O Jati me). 
This mobility dimension connects the Dooshan Panji to larger questions in 
the sociology of Indian caste: the role of the frontier, migration, and political 
disruption (particularly the Tughlaq invasion and the Karnat dynasty's 
displacement) in reshaping caste boundaries and generating new social 
categories. The Shrotriya sub-caste itself — demonstrably created around 
1800 CE through genealogical certification for cash — is the most dramatic

evidence that caste 'tradition' in Mithila was actively manufactured through 
the Panji apparatus. 
IV.4 The Sociology of Knowledge: Honour Killing and 
Institutional Suppression 
The document's most explosive sociological claim concerns the deliberate 
suppression of information from the Dooshan Panji by 20th-century scholars 
— specifically by Ramanath Jha and, subsequently, Udayanath Jha 'Ashok' 
— working within the institutional framework of Sahitya Akademi. Gajendra 
Thakur's editorial framework frames this suppression as an 'honour killing' of 
Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya: the posthumous erasure of a philosopher's Charmakar 
wife and the circumstances of his birth from the authoritative scholarly 
record. 
This sociological claim is specific and documentable. Dinesh Chandra 
Bhattacharya (1958, History of Navya-Nyaya in Mithila) — writing that the 
family connected to Gaṅgeśa 'dwindled into insignificance' and became 
'extinct soon after his son's death' — did so on the basis of information 
provided by Ramanath Jha, who demonstrably knew of the Dooshan Panji 
evidence but withheld it. Bhattacharya further notes that 'the family which 
was inferior in social status is now extinct in Mithila' — a sentence that, read 
against the Dooshan Panji evidence, constitutes a statement about the 
Charmakar lineage connection that Ramanath Jha was unwilling to make 
explicit. 
This is a textbook case of what Robert Merton called the sociology of 
knowledge operating within an institutional framework of power: knowledge 
production shaped not by empirical inadequacy but by caste ideology 
operating through scholarly authority. The Sahitya Akademi monograph by 
Udayanath Jha 'Ashok' (post-2009, therefore with full access to the Dooshan 
Panji evidence released that year) perpetuates this suppression, which Thakur 
documents with precision.

V.3 Genealogical Implications for the Navya-Nyāya Tradition 
The genealogical evidence has direct philosophical-historical implications. 
Gaṅgeśa was posthumously born — this accounts for the absence of 
information about his father in subsequent records and explains Dinesh 
Chandra Bhattacharya's (1958) puzzling comment that 'there is no other 
reference to Gangesa' and that his family 'dwindled into insignificance.' The 
family's subsequent Charmakar connections rendered it genealogically 
'impure' in the dominant Brahmin record — a social marginalisation that the 
Dooshan Panji precisely documents while the main Panji records suppress. 
The transmission history of the Tattvacintāmaṇi illuminates the consequence. 
Pakshadhar Mishra — himself a contemporary of Vidyapati II (distinct from 
the Padavali poet) — refused to allow copies of the Tattvacintāmaṇi to be 
made in Mithila. It was Vasudeva of Bengal, a visiting student who 
memorised the text, and his disciple Raghunath Shiromani who established 
the Navya-Nyāya school at Navadvipa in Bengal. The displacement of 
Navya-Nyāya from Mithila to Bengal — standardly attributed to intellectual 
factors — is revealed by the Dooshan Panji evidence as at least partly a 
consequence of caste-based suppression: 'the extinction of the Navya-Nyāya 
School from Mithila was a revenge of nature against the honour killing of 
Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya and his family.' 
Stephen Phillips (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy, 2020) corroborates 
that genealogical records in Mithila 'suggest that he had a wife and three sons 
and a daughter' — and that Gaṅgeśa's son Vardhamāna describes his father as 
'jagad-guru,' equivalent to 'Distinguished University Professor.' This Stanford 
SEP entry, based on independent scholarship, is consistent with but does not 
reference the Dooshan Panji's fuller evidence — evidence that Gajendra 
Thakur has now placed in the public domain. 
VI. The Dooshan Panji as World Heritage Document 
VI.1 Comparative Genealogical Traditions 
The Dooshan Panji's claim to world significance rests on a comparative 
genealogical argument made explicitly in Gajendra Thakur's editorial 
apparatus: 'In no society in the world has the genealogy of humans spread 
over such a vast geographical area been recorded in this way for more than 1,

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 413 
years as the people of Mithilanchal have continuously preserved — honestly 
recording the education of their ancestors, the so-called attrition, and 
relationship details, artistic sensibility, migration and immigration; this is 
extraordinary.' 
This claim, while expressed with regional pride, has empirical support. The 
closest comparanda are: 
• 
Icelandic Sagas (9th–13th century): genealogically rich but covering a 
much smaller population and geography. 
• 
Chinese Zupu (clan genealogies): vast in scope but far less systematically 
inter-caste in their recording. 
• 
Jewish Yichus (pedigree) records: emphasise scholarly and rabbinic 
lineage but do not document transgressive unions. 
• 
European nobility peerage records (Burke's Peerage, Almanach de 
Gotha): extensive but focused on elite lineages only. 
The Mithila Panji system is exceptional in combining: (a) a span of 1,500+ 
years; (b) comprehensive coverage of an entire regional Brahmin 
community; (c) structured documentation of transgressive unions alongside 
normative genealogy; and (d) a mechanism for tracking cross-generational 
restoration of status. No comparable archive exists in world genealogical 
tradition. 
VI.2 The Epistemological Status of the Panji 
From a Navya-Nyāya epistemological standpoint — appropriately invoked 
given Gaṅgeśa's own connection to the document — the Panji constitutes a 
form of Shabda pramāṇa (testimony-based knowledge). Its reliability rests on 
the institutional continuity of the Panjikar families and the cross-referencing 
between multiple registers (Mool, Gotra, Patra, Dooshan, Devānand). The 
transliteration work undertaken by Gajendra Thakur from 2000 CE onward 
— and the publication of 11, palm-leaf images — constitutes a crucial 
epistemic upgrade: the transformation of oral/inscribed testimony into 
digitally verifiable evidence. 
The academic use of the Panji evidence by Anshuman Pandey (2014) and its 
acknowledgement by Stephen Phillips (2020) in the Stanford Encyclopedia 
of Philosophy represent stages in the authorisation of this knowledge within 
Western academic epistemology — a process the Dooshan Panji itself would

recognise as a form of Shuddhi: the progressive validation of knowledge 
previously held to be impure or inadmissible. 
VII. Political and Ethical Dimensions 
VII.1 Caste Violence in Scholarship 
The Dooshan Panji document is not merely an archival object but an active 
site of scholarly contestation. Gajendra Thakur's framing — the 'honour 
killing' of Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya — deploys a contemporary vocabulary of 
caste-based violence to describe what is, in scholarly terms, a case of 
epistemic suppression: the deliberate withholding of genealogical evidence 
that would complicate the dominant, upper-caste narrative of Navya-Nyāya's 
founding figure. 
The Sahitya Akademi's institutional role in this suppression deserves specific 
attention. As Gajendra Thakur documents, the Bhasha Samman awarded to 
Udayanath Jha 'Ashok' by the same body that published his monograph on 
Gaṅgeśa creates a circular system of validation in which the suppression of 
uncomfortable evidence is rewarded rather than corrected. This is not merely 
an academic dispute but a political economy of knowledge in which caste 
ideology operates through institutional channels. 
VII.2 Dalit and Subaltern Stakes 
The Dooshan Panji's explicit documentation of Brahmin men's unions with 
Charmakar (Dalit), Rajak, devadasi, and Muslim women — and the 
genealogical tracking of their descendants — constitutes primary evidence 
for the historical presence and social participation of subaltern communities 
in Mithila's cultural and intellectual life. The mother of Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya 
was a Charmakāriṇī; his wife was a Charmakāriṇī. The Tattvacintāmaṇi — 
perhaps the most sophisticated work of logical epistemology in pre-modern 
world philosophy — was composed by a man whose family was by Panji 
standards genealogically compromised. 
This has implications for Dalit intellectual history. The recovery of this 
evidence through the Dooshan Panji release (2009) and its inclusion in 
Gajendra

published research 
(including

short 
story 
'Shabdashastram,' translated as 'The Science of Words,' Indian Literature,

VII.3 Mithila's Tolerance and Adaptive Social Logic 
The document's editorial apparatus makes a broader sociological argument: 
that Mithila's society, despite being superficially categorised by the 'Jaivara' 
designation (implying caste privilege), demonstrated a historically 
documented pattern of tolerance, reform-openness, and non-permanent caste 
hostility. The Brahmin of Mithila, as the document argues, was 'more liberal 
and flexible compared to the other civilisations and cultures of India from 
ancient times; there was no permanent religious bigotry here. The scope for 
reform was continuously kept open.' 
Whether one accepts this self-assessment or regards it with the critical 
distance appropriate to community self-representation, the Dooshan Panji 
evidence provides empirical grounding for at least a modified version of the 
claim: that Mithila's caste system was characterised by a managed fluidity 
unavailable under the rigidly endogamous model that colonial census-taking 
and subsequent scholarship imposed on Indian social history. 
VIII. Methodological Reflections 
VIII.1 Limits of Textual Extraction 
This research report is based on the Mithilakshar-to-Devanagari 
transliteration of the Dooshan Panji and its English-language framing by 
Gajendra Thakur. The primary source (palm-leaf and bark manuscripts) is 
available digitally but requires specialised palaeographic expertise in 
Mithilakshar script for direct verification. Tables 1–97 present data in a 
compressed genealogical format in which individual identities are rendered 
as abbreviated syllables arranged in columnar trees — a format that reflects 
the oral and inscribed traditions of Panjikar practice rather than the 
conventions of modern scholarship. 
Future research should engage directly with: (a) the Mithilakshar originals; 
(b) the Panjikar family traditions of interpretation; (c) the full Devānand 
Panji corpus (of which only selected entries are reproduced here); and (d) the 
archaeological and epigraphic record of the Karnat dynasty and early Mithila, 
against which the Panji's dating claims can be independently verified.

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 416 
VIII.2 Archive, Memory, and Community 
The Panji system raises fundamental questions about the relationship 
between community and archive. As Pierre Nora argues in his analysis of 
lieux de mémoire (sites of memory), the shift from milieu de mémoire 
(environments of memory, where memory is lived) to lieux de mémoire 
(where memory is deliberately preserved and commemorated) signals the 
death of living memory and the birth of history as archive. The Mithila Panji 
system occupies an intermediate position: it is an archive that has remained, 
for centuries, a living institution — a milieu de mémoire that has also always 
been a lieu de mémoire. 
The digital release of 2009 — the transformation of palm-leaf images into 
downloadable JPEGs and Devanagari text on videha.co.in — represents a 
second-order archivisation: the archive of an archive, the inscription of a 
living genealogical system into the public record of global digital culture. 
This 
creates 
new 
possibilities 
for 
research 
and 
new 
risks 
of 
decontextualisation that future scholarship must navigate carefully. 
IX. Conclusions 
The Dooshan Panji is a document of exceptional world significance. Its 
contributions span multiple disciplines: 
• 
In genealogical studies: it constitutes perhaps the most comprehensive 
inter-caste genealogical archive in world history, spanning 1, years and 
covering a regional population in extraordinary detail. 
• 
In anthropology: it demonstrates the dynamic, processual, and negotiated 
character of caste in pre-colonial Mithila, challenging static colonial 
models and revealing sophisticated mechanisms for managing social 
transgression and restoring lineage status. 
• 
In sociology: it exposes the political economy of knowledge production 
in which caste ideology operates through institutional channels (Sahitya 
Akademi) to suppress primary source evidence inconvenient to dominant 
social narratives. 
• 
In the history of philosophy: it provides primary genealogical evidence 
for the social origins of Gaṅgeśa Upādhyāya and his family, with direct 
implications for the history of the Navya-Nyāya school and the 
displacement of that tradition from Mithila to Bengal.

records.label: पञ्जी PDF 14 — खण्ड 1 (block 350–690, IAST)
records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 417 
• 
In gender studies: it reveals a gendered double inscription — the 
systematic erasure of women's names and social identities from main 
genealogical registers while their presence is preserved in the suppressed 
Dooshan register. 
• 
In Dalit intellectual history: it provides primary evidence for the 
contribution of Charmakar, devadasi, and other subaltern communities to 
the social and intellectual history of Mithila. 
The decision by Gajendra Thakur and the Videha Parallel Literature 
Movement to release this archive publicly in 2009 — and to place it in the 
context of a sustained critique of Sahitya Akademi's institutional casteism — 
represents an act of what we might call archival activism: the deployment of 
genealogical evidence in the service of a more inclusive and more truthful 
history of Mithila and Indian intellectual culture. 
The Dooshan Panji deserves recognition as a primary source document of 
global scholarly significance and as a model of community-driven archival 
preservation. Its systematic study — with the full apparatus of 
anthropological, sociological, genealogical, palaeographic, and philosophical 
method — is among the most important scholarly tasks facing the field of 
Maithili and Mithila studies in the coming decades.

BeejiPurush: The foundational ancestral patriarch from whom a primary

AswajanapatraPanji (Siddhanta Patra): The formal, legally binding 
"Certificate of Non-Relationship" issued by a certified Panjikar, without 
which a marriage is declared uncanonical (Ashastriya). 
DooshanPanji: The specialized register of systemic lineage fractures, 
biological intermixtures, and social status degradation. 
Unions with Unknown/Exotic Groups:The text registers that Chamu 
(Valiyas Mool), Gangadhar, Nathu, and Wisho experienced lines of descent 
where daughters or mothers were of completely unknown extractions (Agnat 
Vansh/Jati). 
Unions with Non-Brahmin Communities:The Panji logs explicit unions 
with lower socio-economic or non-Brahmin communities, such as 
connections involving a Charmakar (leatherworker community, e.g., the 
lineage of Anand, 211/1), Rajak (washerman community, e.g., Dharam, 
207/1), and Tailik (oil-presser community, e.g., Devai, 194/11). 
Indigenous Royal Outgroups:It logs instances like the marriage of 
Chandrapath’s daughter into theKoch Rajvanshi royal family of 
Assam(Princess Sohago, 208/1). 
Lineages associated with prominent figures like Visho (Sarisab Mool), 
Ratanu (Ghusaut Mool), and Madhav (Budhwal Mool) contain historical 
documentation of maternal roots linked to the Devadasi lineage systems. 
These children were completely absorbed into the social fabric, and the 
registry carefully tracks their lines over several generations until their line 
achieved complete purification (typically by the 6th generation, as 
demonstrated in the lineage of Devadhar, who attained full lineage 
normalization). 
Post-Mortem Overlaps:It explicitly records that the celebrated Navya-
Nyaya philosopherGangesha Upadhyaya(author of the Tattvacintamani) was 
born 
five 
years 
after 
the 
recorded 
death 
of 
his 
legal 
father 
(*PitruParokshePancha VarshaVyatiteGangeshotpatti*). 
The "Taskar" Designation:In another instance, when Pandit Keshav Jha of 
Mangarouni willfully intercepted and rescued a bride originally selected for 
King Lakshmishwar Singh—and married her through a forced runtime 
Kanyadaan—the furious monarch officially summoned the Panjikars and 
legally ordered them to prefix the titleTaskar(Thief) permanently to Keshav 
Jha’s lineage in the state records. 
Shrotriya(Soiti):The elite ritual tier, categorized into8 distinct structural 
subdivisions 
(Chaugola/Categories)and 
managed 
through32 
primaryShrotriyaPanjis. Their lineages were termedLaukit.

Yogya (Jog):The intermediate rank assigned to families with high 
educational merit and scholastic background, but lacking the supreme ritual 
purity of the Shrotriyas. 
Panjibaddh(Jaibar/ Vanshaja):The base tier comprising the general 
population of verified, registered lines. This segment was tracked 
through153 distinct registries spanning 15 functional societal ranks. 
High-status lineages (particularly top-tier Shrotriyas or highly ranked Yogya 
lines) often faced financial distress. 
Conversely, lower-tier families (*Panjibaddh/Jaibar*) frequently accumulated 
substantial capital through agricultural expansion, administrative positions, or 
trade. 
The Ghataks brokered hypergamous unions between wealthy lower-tier 
brides and highly ranked, financially strained grooms. 
To institutionalize this transformation, substantial financial transactions were 
directed to the Panjikars under the guise of traveling expenses (Marga Vyaya) 
and registration premiums. 
Gotra Exclusivity:The bride and groom cannot belong to the same paternal 
Gotra. 
Pravara Exclusivity:They cannot share identical ancestral sages (Pravara). 
MaternalVimataRule:The bride cannot be a direct descendant of the groom's 
stepmother's brother.

Temporal Anomaly of Paternity:The registry contains an ancient scribal 
note stating that Gangesha was born five years after the passing of his father: 
Inter-Caste Marriage:The ledger records that Gangesha’s wife, Medha, was 
a Charmakarini (a woman from the untouchable leather-tanning/cobbler 
community).1 
Generation 1:Gunawati Himsha marries Chamaru of the *ValiyasaMool*, 
producing a son named Gangadhar.1 
Generation 2:Gangadhar’s daughter is married to Nathu of the 
*SarisavMool*, producing a son named Visho.1 
Generation 3:Visho’s daughter is married to Gangu of the *SakaradhiMool*, 
producing a son named Goge.1 
Generation 4:Goge’s daughter marries Kanhu of the *PanichobhMool*, 
producing a son named Rudra.1 
Generation 5:Rudra’s daughter marries Helu of the *DariharaMool, 
producing a son named Chand, whose daughter subsequently marries Horai of 
the Pali Mool*, producing a son named Ram.1 
Generation 6:Ram’s daughter is married to Chand of the Narwal Mool, and 
their son, Devadhar, is declared completely pure (Purnatah Shuddha).1 
Extreme Polygamy:High-status Bikaua men turned marriage into a primary 
profession, marrying between twenty-five and thirty women from lower-
status families to extract continuous cash payments.15 
Residential Abandonment:The Bikaua grooms typically abandoned their 
lower-status wives in their natal homes immediately after the ceremony.15 
The wives remained with their own parents, where the groom would visit 
once a year or whenever he sought to extract more money.6 
The Crisis of Mass Widowhood:Because a single Bikaua groom was 
married to dozens of women, his death instantly rendered a vast number of 
women—many of whom were child brides—widows.15 These widows were 
condemned to lives of severe social deprivation and isolation in their paternal 
households, as remarriage was strictly forbidden.15 
The Starting Lineage:The registers officially begin by mapping 
theShandilya Gotra, specifically starting with theGangoliMool. 
The First Ancestor (Aadi Purush):The very first foundational figure 
recorded in this block isGangadhar. 
The First Generation:The record mathematically maps out that from one of 
Gangadhar's marriages, he had exactly two offspring (sons):Veer 
JhaandNarayan.

ADDENDUM 
Structured Data Tables and Genealogical Archive 
 
This addendum presents the structured genealogical data underlying the research in 
this volume. The data is drawn from six source archives: the Prācīn Pañjī, the 
Nagram Khaṇḍa 3, the Uṭīp Khaṇḍa 2, the Dūṣaṇa Pañjī, the Pañjī Vol. II, and the 
Gotra-Mūla-Mūlagrāma thesaurus compiled from Prelim-a, Prelim-b, and Puñjī-01 
(Diwali 2014). All text has been rendered in standard IAST transliteration 
throughout. 
Master Gotra × Mūla × Mūlagrāma Table 
The table below consolidates the primary Gotra, Mūla, and Mūlagrāma records of 
Maithil Brahmin genealogy. Each entry identifies the ancestral clan (Gotra), the root 
lineage (Mūla), its founding village (Mūlagrāma), the founding ancestor (Bījī 
Puruṣa), and the social classification. The seven major Gotras are Śāṇḍilya, Vatsa, 
Kāśyapa, Pārāśara, Sāvarṇa, Bhāradvāja, and Kātyāyana. 
Gotra-Mūla Thesaurus: Classical and Laukika Names 
Classical (Maulik) Mūla names are the canonical designations recorded in the Pañjī. 
Laukika (colloquial) variants are the forms in everyday usage. For example, 
Khaṇḍavala (classical) is also known as Khadaur; Gangoli as Ganguliwar; Sodarpura 
as Sodarpuriye. The thesaurus records 43 Mūlas under Śāṇḍilya, 40 under Vatsa, 27 
under Kāśyapa. 
Notable Persons Index 
Key scholars and historical figures documented in the Pañjī records include: Gonū 
Jhā (Dhūrtarāja, M.M.U., 1050–1150 CE), Vidyāpati Ṭhākura (Mahākavi, 1350–
1435 CE), Āryabhaṭṭa (Scientist, 476–550 CE, 39th generation in Maṇḍar Mūla), 
Gaṃgeśa Upādhyāya (Navya-Nyāya founder, Tattvacintāmaṇi, c.1200–1280 CE), 
Lakṣimā Ṭhakurā'ini (Dharmaśāstri, 13th–14th c. CE), and Guṇākara Jhā (first 
Pañjikāra, appointed 1326 CE).

records.text:
Decoding the Panji of Mithila 
Gajendra Thakur — 428 
Genealogical Terminology 
Suta / Sutā / Sutau: Son / Daughter or sons (plural) / Two sons 
Dau (Dauhitra): Daughter's son; maternal grandson 
Ddau (Daudauhitra): Daughter's daughter's son 
Mūla: Root lineage; original ancestral village designation 
Mūlagrāma: Specific village of residence at time of Pañjī recording 
Bījī Puruṣa: Seed-ancestor; earliest known founder of a Mūla 
Gotra: Patrilineal clan traced to a Vedic sage (Ṛṣi) 
Pravara: Sub-classification identifying Ṛgvedic sage ancestors within a Gotra 
Uṭheṛha: 32-ancestor document mapping kinship nodes for marriage verification 
Ṣaṣṭhī: 16 specific kinship positions checked for marriage eligibility 
Siddhānta Patra: Certificate of non-kinship (Asvajanya Patra) issued by Pañjikāra 
Dūṣaṇa Pañjī: The Black Book: register of genealogical deviations and transgressions 
Mahamahopadhyāya: Highest scholarly title; greatest of great teachers 
Pañjikāra: Authorized genealogist; keeper of Pañjī records 
Śrotriya: Highest-ranked Maithil Brahmin category (Pubaripar) 
Yogya / Pañjibaddha: Second and third tier Brahmin classifications (Pachhbaripar) 
Sapiṇḍa: Kinship within prohibited degrees; basis of exogamy rules 
Gramarambha: Opening of a village record in the Pañjī 
Upaṭīp (U.T.): Sub-note; supplementary annotation to the main Pañjī register 
Sāmūha-Lekhya: Pre-1326 informal genealogical record system
